docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
Loom is a lifecycle management framework for Roblox.

It is developed because a popular alternative Knit is no longer maintained.

If you think that anything is missing or incorrect, feel free to open an issue or submit a pull request on GitHub.

docs.title: AddSource()
docs.text:
Luau 
Loom.AddSource(
    source: Instance
)

Parameters:

  • source Instance — Source that you want to add.

Loom is going to include this source to be used Loom.Run() if it's a ModuleScript, otherwise it is going to iterate through descendants and include all found ModuleScripts.

Will fail if Loom is already running.

docs.title: Run()
docs.text:
Luau 
Loom.Run()

Starts all services included by Loom.AddSource().

Will fail if Loom is already running.

docs.title: Terminate()
docs.text:
Luau 
Loom.Terminate()

Stops all services included by Loom.AddSource().

Will fail if Loom is not running.

docs.title: IsRunning()
docs.text:
Luau 
Loom.IsRunning(): boolean

Returns:

  • boolean — A value representing if Loom is running or not.

docs.title: CreateService()
docs.text:
Luau 
Loom.CreateService(
    table: Loom.ServiceTable
)

Parameters:

  • table Loom.ServiceTable — Table that you want to register as a service.

docs.title: OnStart()
docs.text:
Luau 
Loom.OnStart(
    service: Loom.ServiceTable,
    func: Loom.EventHandler
)

Parameters:

  • service Loom.ServiceTable — A table that has been marked as a service using Loom.CreateService, to which you want to attach the lifecycle event.
  • func Loom.EventHandler — A function that is going to be called when this event is triggered.

docs.title: OnStop()
docs.text:
Luau 
Loom.OnStop(
    service: Loom.ServiceTable,
    func: Loom.EventHandler
)

Parameters:

  • service Loom.ServiceTable — A table that has been marked as a service using Loom.CreateService, to which you want to attach the lifecycle event.
  • func Loom.EventHandler — A function that is going to be called when this event is triggered.

docs.title: On()
docs.text:
Luau 
Loom.On(
    service: Loom.ServiceTable,
    trigger: Loom.Trigger,
    func: Loom.EventHandler
)

Parameters:

  • service Loom.ServiceTable — A table that has been marked as a service using Loom.CreateService, to which you want to attach the lifecycle event.
  • trigger Loom.Trigger — The event on which the handler function is going to be triggered.
  • func Loom.EventHandler — A function that is going to be called when this event is triggered.
Deprecated

Loom.On() is deprecated as of v1.1.2 and will be removed in v1.2.

Use specific OnStart() and OnStop() functions instead.

docs.title: Triggers
docs.text:
Deprecated

Triggers are deprecated as of v1.1.2 and will be removed in v1.2.

docs.title: IsServiceInitialized()
docs.text:
Luau 
Loom.IsServiceInitialized(
    service: Loom.ServiceTable
): boolean
Deprecated

Loom.IsServiceInitialized() is deprecated as of v1.1.2 and will be removed in v1.2.

You can initialize your modules without any library-specific code in Luau.

Parameters:

  • service Loom.ServiceTable — A table that has been marked as a service using Loom.CreateService, to which you want to attach the lifecycle event.

Returns:

  • boolean — A value representing if the given service is initialized or not.

docs.title: IsServiceStarted()
docs.text:
Luau 
Loom.IsServiceStarted(
    service: Loom.ServiceTable
): boolean

Parameters:

  • service Loom.ServiceTable — A table that has been marked as a service using Loom.CreateService, to which you want to attach the lifecycle event.

Returns:

  • boolean — A value representing if the given service is started or not.

docs.title: Trigger
docs.text:
Deprecated

Loom.Trigger is deprecated as of v1.1.2 and will be removed in v1.2.

Use specific OnStart() and OnStop() functions instead.

docs.title: Getting started
docs.text:
This tutorial provides a basic example of using Loom.

We are going to write a simple service that is going to log players that connect and disconnect from the server.

docs.title: Writing the server script
docs.text:
Automatic module loading

Loom can detect and load it's services automatically.

That means that if you reference an Instance when calling Loom.AddSource(), it is going to loop through all child instances, and load all found ModuleScripts as loom services.

Luau 
local ReplicatedStorage = game:GetService("ReplicatedStorage")
local ServerScriptService = game:GetService("ServerScriptService")
local Loom = require(ReplicatedStorage.Packages.Loom)

Loom.AddSource(ServerScriptService:WaitForChild("Services"))

Loom.Run()

game:BindToClose(Loom.Terminate)

docs.title: Registering the service
docs.text:
Now, let's create a basic service. You might have noticed that in the server script, I have added the ServerScriptService.Services folder as a source. That means that all ModuleScripts under that folder are going to be loaded by Loom. That's why I am creating a PlayerLogService under ServerScriptService.Services.

Luau 
local ReplicatedStorage = game:GetService("ReplicatedStorage")
local Loom = require(ReplicatedStorage.Packages.Loom)

local PlayerLogService = {}

Loom.CreateService(PlayerLogService)

return PlayerLogService

As you can see, we have to mark our table as a Loom service. That is going to allow us to add lifecycle events and load this module automatically.

docs.title: Writing the service functions
docs.text:
We are going to write the service functions just like we would in a normal codebase

Luau 
--- ...

function PlayerLogService.LogPlayerAdded(player: Player)
    print(player.Name.." has joined the game")
end

function PlayerLogService.LogPlayerRemoving(player: Player)
    print(player.Name.." has left the game")
end

--- ...

docs.title: Writing the service lifecycle
docs.text:
To add the service lifecycle, we are going to use Loom functions that act as annotations for our module.

Luau 
Loom.OnStart(PlayerLogService, function()
    print("PlayerLogService has started!")
end)

Loom.OnStop(PlayerLogService, function()
    print("PlayerLogService has stopped!")
end)

docs.title: Connecting and cleaning up events
docs.text:
Now, let's conenct our functions to our events, and make sure that we don't forget the cleanup logic to allow us to start and stop services dynamically

Luau 
local PlayerLogService = {}

PlayerLogService.PlayerAdded = nil :: RBXScriptConnection?
PlayerLogService.PlayerRemoving = nil :: RBXScriptConnection?

-- ...

Loom.OnStart(PlayerLogService, function()
    PlayerLogService.PlayerAdded = Players.PlayerAdded:Connect(PlayerLogService.LogPlayerAdded)
    PlayerLogService.PlayerRemoving = Players.PlayerRemoving:Connect(PlayerLogService.LogPlayerRemoving)

    print("PlayerLogService has started!")
end)

Loom.OnStop(PlayerLogService, function()
    if PlayerLogService.PlayerAdded then
        PlayerLogService.PlayerAdded:Disconnect()
    end

    if PlayerLogService.PlayerRemoving then
        PlayerLogService.PlayerRemoving:Disconnect()
    end

    print("PlayerLogService has stopped!")
end)

-- ...

Finally, let's launch our game, and take a look at the output

19:43:13.296  PlayerLogService has initialized!  -  Server - PlayerLogService:14
19:43:13.296  PlayerLogService has started!  -  Server - PlayerLogService:21
19:43:13.371  wireofparadise has joined the game  -  Server - PlayerLogService:37
19:43:23.474  wireofparadise has left the game  -  Server - PlayerLogService:41
19:43:23.474  PlayerLogService has stopped!  -  Server - PlayerLogService:33

As you can see, everything works correctly:

  • Our service is initialized
  • Our service is started
  • My character joins the game
  • My character leaves the game, forcing the server to stop
  • Server closes and stops all services subsequently

Now you should be able to write your own services, good luck!

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
wallyrbxm

Add the following line to your wally.toml:

Loom = "wireofparadise/loom@1.1.3"

And then reinstall your packages, using the following command:

wally install

Installing Loom as a standalone roblox model:

Navigate to the latest release on GitHub, download the Loom.rbxm or Loom.rbxmx file, and insert it into your place.
